title: Principles of Emergency Care & BLS - Enumeration & Identification Quiz

questions.question: How many Emergency Action Principles are there?

The five Emergency Action Principles are: 1. Survey the scene (Check), 2. Activate medical assistance (Call), 3. _____ (Care/Check ABCs), 4. Secondary Assessment (Head-to-toe exam), and 5. Transport/Definitive Care.
The five Emergency Action Principles include: Survey the scene, _____, Primary Assessment, Secondary Assessment, and Transport/Definitive Care.
What is the fourth Emergency Action Principle?
What is the fifth Emergency Action Principle?

The Principles of Emergency Care include: 1. Do no further harm, 2. _____ (treat the most life-threatening first), 3. Seek consent, 4. Maintain airway, breathing, and circulation, and 5. Reassure the victim.
The Principles of Emergency Care include: Do no further harm, Triage, _____, Maintain airway, breathing, and circulation, and Reassure the victim.
What is the fourth Principle of Emergency Care?

When activating medical assistance, the information to remember includes: 1. Your location, 2. The _____ of the emergency/injury, 3. The victim's condition and age, 4. Your name and contact number, and 5. Do not hang up until the dispatcher tells you to.
When activating medical assistance, what should you NOT do until the dispatcher tells you to?
AID in Initial Response stands for: Assess the scene, Identify the problem, and _____.
In the Golden Rules of Emergency Care (DON'Ts), what should you NOT give to an unconscious victim?
In the Golden Rules of Emergency Care (DON'Ts), what should you NOT insert into the victim's mouth?
In the Golden Rules of Emergency Care (DON'Ts), what should you NOT do to the victim except to get help?

The Golden Rules of Emergency Care (DON'Ts) include: 1. DO NOT move the victim unless in immediate danger, 2. DO NOT give _____ to an unconscious victim, 3. DO NOT insert any objects into the victim's mouth, 4. DO NOT leave the victim alone, 5. DO NOT remove _____ of an injured victim unless necessary.
The steps in the CAB-D approach are: C - _____, A - Airway, B - Breathing, D - Defibrillation.
In the CAB-D approach, what does 'D' stand for?
The basic steps in Hands-Only CPR during infectious outbreaks are: 1. Check responsiveness, 2. _____ (Call 911/117), 3. Start chest compressions, 4. Use an AED if available.
In Hands-Only CPR during infectious outbreaks, what is skipped to avoid disease transmission?
List three indications of CPR.
The indications of CPR are: Unconsciousness, _____, and No pulse.
The Primary Assessment components (ABCDE) are: A - Airway, B - _____, C - Circulation, D - Disability/Deficit, E - Exposure/Environment.
In the Primary Assessment (ABCDE), what does 'D' stand for?
In the Primary Assessment (ABCDE), what does 'E' stand for?
The SAMPLE history questions in Secondary Assessment are: S - Signs/Symptoms, A - _____, M - Medications, P - Past medical history, L - Last oral intake, E - Events.
The SAMPLE history questions in Secondary Assessment are: S - Signs/Symptoms, A - Allergies, M - _____, P - Past medical history, L - Last oral intake, E - Events.
In the DCAP-BTLS head-to-toe assessment: D - Deformities, C - Contusions, A - Abrasions, P - _____, B - Burns, T - Tenderness, L - Lacerations, S - Swelling.
In the DCAP-BTLS head-to-toe assessment, what does 'T' stand for?
In the DCAP-BTLS head-to-toe assessment, what does 'S' stand for?
The acronym STOPSS indicates when to stop CPR. What does the first 'S' stand for?

The STOPSS mnemonic for when to stop CPR includes: S - Spontaneous breathing/circulation resumes, T - The rescuer is exhausted, O - Ordered/Relieved by higher authority, P - _____ declares the victim dead, S - Scene becomes unsafe, S - Signs of death are obvious.
What does ROSC stand for?
The emergency principle that requires checking if the area is safe for the rescuer, victim, and bystanders is called:
What is the emergency principle that involves calling EMS and starting CPR simultaneously?
What is the acronym used to remember patient history during secondary assessment?
What is the maneuver used to relieve severe airway obstruction in adults?
What is the law in the Philippines that mandates CPR training in schools and workplaces?
What is the recommended chest compression rate per minute during CPR?
What is the recommended depth of chest compressions in adults?
What is the emergency breathing rate for adults (seconds per breath)?
What artery is used to check the pulse in infants during rescue breathing?
What position is used when a victim starts breathing normally after CPR?
What acronym is used to assess responsiveness (Alert, Voice, Pain, Unresponsive)?
What is the emergency hotline number for Oro Rescue in Cagayan de Oro City?
What is the technique of tilting the head and lifting the chin to open the airway called?
What drug is used by medical services to stimulate a stopped or severely slowed heart?
